region_groups.name: Southeast
region_groups.description: The humid subtropical and mixed southeastern U.S., from the Mid-South through the Atlantic and Gulf coasts—long warm seasons, strong fruit and row-crop production.

regions.name: Virginia
regions.description: Virginia stretches from cool mountain west to mild Tidewater. Warm-season produce ripens earlier in coastal areas; apples and fall crops dominate western valleys.

regions.name: West Virginia
regions.description: West Virginia’s hills and elevation create a shorter warm season than neighbors, with strong apple, squash, and cool-weather vegetable traditions.

regions.name: North Carolina
regions.description: North Carolina spans cool highlands to subtropical coast—sweet potatoes, tobacco heritage, summer berries, and early spring produce vary sharply by zone.

regions.name: South Carolina
regions.description: South Carolina’s long growing season supports peaches, collards, summer vegetables, and coastal seafood with an early spring relative to the Northeast.

regions.name: Georgia
regions.description: Georgia leads in pecans and peanuts, with famous peaches and Vidalia onions; southern zones push warm-season crops earlier.

regions.name: Florida
regions.description: Florida’s mild winters and long humid summers create unique calendars—citrus, winter vegetables in the peninsula, and tropical fruit in the south.

regions.name: Alabama
regions.description: Alabama blends Gulf warmth and Appalachian foothills—watermelons, peanuts, sweet potatoes, and long summers for field crops.

regions.name: Mississippi
regions.description: Mississippi’s hot summers favor soybeans, catfish, sweet potatoes, and summer vegetables, with earlier harvests than northern states.

regions.name: Tennessee
regions.description: Tennessee runs from cool eastern mountains to warmer west—distinct berry and orchard belts with Middle Tennessee stone fruit.

regions.name: Kentucky
regions.description: Kentucky balances row crops with burley tobacco heritage, cattle, and seasonal vegetables—spring arrives earlier than the upper Midwest.

regions.name: Arkansas
regions.description: Arkansas offers long summers for rice, soybeans, and truck crops; local markets highlight berries, watermelons, and fall sweet potatoes.

regions.name: Louisiana
regions.description: Louisiana’s long humid season supports rice, crawfish, sugarcane, and early spring greens—summer heat peaks vegetable harvest before northern neighbors.
